questionText: Three identical resistors are connected in series. When a certain potential difference is applied across the combination, the total power dissipated is 27 W. What power would be dissipated if the three resistors were connected in parallel across the same potential difference?

(a) Calculate the equivalent resistance of the circuit of Figure 19.72 between $x$ and $y$. (b) If a voltmeter is connected between points $a$ and $x$ when the current in the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor is 2.4 A in the direction from left to right in the figure, what will it read?

parts.label: a
parts.question: Calculate the equivalent resistance of the circuit of Figure 19.72 between $x$ and $y$.

parts.label: b
parts.question: If a voltmeter is connected between points $a$ and $x$ when the current in the $8.0\,\Omega$ resistor is 2.4 A in the direction from left to right in the figure, what will it read?

questionText: A power plant transmits $150\,\mathrm{kW}$ of power to a nearby town through wires that have a total resistance of 0.25 Ω. What percentage of the power is dissipated as heat in the wire if the power is transmitted at (a) 220 V and (b) 22 kV?

parts.label: b
parts.question: 22 kV

questionText: What must the emf $\mathcal{E}$ in Figure 19.73 be in order for the current through the $7.00\,\Omega$ resistor to be 1.80 A? Each emf source has negligible internal resistance.
expectedAnswer: a) 9.98 × 10^3 J  b) 5.54 × 10^3 J  c) Isobaric  d) Isobaric (Q = 2.49 × 10^4 J vs Q = 0)

questionText: For the circuit shown in Figure 19.74, if a voltmeter is connected across points $a$ and $b$, (a) what will it read, and (b) which point is at a higher potential, $a$ or $b$?

parts.label: a
parts.question: what will it read

parts.label: b
parts.question: which point is at a higher potential, $a$ or $b$?

questionText: A 4600 Ω resistor is connected across a charged 0.800 nF capacitor. The initial current through the resistor, just after the connection is made, is measured to be 0.250 A. (a) What magnitude of charge was initially on each plate of this capacitor? (b) How long after the connection is made will it take before the charge is reduced to $1 / e$ of its maximum value?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What magnitude of charge was initially on each plate of this capacitor?

parts.label: b
parts.question: How long after the connection is made will it take before the charge is reduced to $1 / e$ of its maximum value?

questionText: A capacitor that is initially uncharged is connected in series with a resistor and a $400.0\mathrm{V}$ emf source with negligible internal resistance. Just after the circuit is completed, the current through the resistor is $0.800\mathrm{mA}$ and the time constant for the circuit is $6.00\mathrm{s}$. What are (a) the resistance of the resistor and (b) the capacitance of the capacitor?

parts.label: a
parts.question: the resistance of the resistor

parts.label: b
parts.question: the capacitance of the capacitor

questionText: In the circuit shown in Figure 19.75, $R$ is a variable resistor whose value can range from 0 to $\infty$, and $a$ and $b$ are the terminals of a battery having an emf $\mathcal{E} = 15.0\mathrm{V}$ and an internal resistance of $4.00\Omega$. The ammeter and voltmeter are both idealized meters. As $R$ varies over its full range of values, what will be the largest and smallest readings of (a) the voltmeter and (b) the ammeter? (c) Sketch qualitative graphs of the readings of both meters as functions of $R$, as $R$ ranges from 0 to $\infty$.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the largest and smallest readings of the voltmeter

parts.label: b
parts.question: the largest and smallest readings of the ammeter

parts.label: c
parts.question: Sketch qualitative graphs of the readings of both meters as functions of $R$, as $R$ ranges from 0 to $\infty$.

questionText: What is the best explanation for the behavior exhibited in the data?
choices: Longer threads can carry more current than shorter threads and so make better electrical conductors.
The thread stops being a conductor when it is stretched to 13 mm due to breaks that occur in the thin coating.
As the thread is stretched, the coating thins and its resistance increases; as the thread is relaxed, the coating returns nearly to its original state.
The resistance of the thread increases with distance from the end of the thread.

questionText: If the conductivity of the thread results from the aqueous coating only, how does the cross-sectional area $A$ of the coating compare when the thread is $13\mathrm{mm}$ long versus the starting length of $5\mathrm{mm}$? Assume that the resistivity of the coating remains constant and the coating is uniform along the thread. $A_{13\mathrm{mm}}$ is about
What is the maximum current that flows in the thread during this experiment if the voltage source is a $9\mathrm{V}$ battery?

In another experiment, a piece of the web is suspended so that it can move freely. When either a positively charged object or a negatively charged object is brought near the web, the thread is observed to move toward the charged object. What is the best interpretation of this observation? The web is
choices: either a positively or negatively charged conductor.

questionText: Assume that a typical open ion channel spanning an axon's membrane has a resistance of $1 \times 10^{11} \Omega$. We can model this ion channel, with its pore, as a $12 \mathrm{~nm}$-long cylinder of radius $0.3 \mathrm{~nm}$. What is the resistivity of the fluid in the pore?

In a simple model of an axon conducting a nerve signal, ions move across the cell membrane through open ion channels, which act as purely resistive elements. If a typical current density (current per unit cross-sectional area) in the cell membrane is $5\mathrm{mA} / \mathrm{cm}^2$ when the voltage across the membrane (the action potential) is $50\mathrm{mV}$, what is the number density of open ion channels in the membrane?

Cell membranes across a wide variety of organisms have a capacitance per unit area of $1\mu \mathrm{F} / \mathrm{cm}^2$. For the electrical signal in a nerve to propagate down the axon, the charge on the membrane "capacitor" must change. What time constant is required when the ion channels are open?

In a $1.25\mathrm{T}$ magnetic field directed vertically upward, a particle having a charge of magnitude $8.50\mu \mathrm{C}$ and initially moving northward at $4.75\mathrm{km / s}$ is deflected toward the east. (a) What is the sign of the charge of this particle? Make a sketch to illustrate how you found your answer. (b) Find the magnetic force on the particle.

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the sign of the charge of this particle? Make a sketch to illustrate how you found your answer.

parts.label: b
parts.question: Find the magnetic force on the particle.

questionText: An ion having charge $+6e$ is traveling horizontally to the left at $8.50\mathrm{km / s}$ when it enters a magnetic field that is perpendicular to its velocity and deflects it downward with an initial magnetic force of $6.94\times 10^{-15}\mathrm{N}$. What are the direction and magnitude of this field? Illustrate your method of solving this problem with a diagram.
expectedAnswer: 0.850 T, directed toward the top of the page

questionText: A proton traveling at $3.60\mathrm{km / s}$ suddenly enters a uniform magnetic field of 0.750 T, traveling at an angle of $55.0^{\circ}$ with the field lines (Figure 20.55). (a) Find the magnitude and direction of the force this magnetic field exerts on the proton. (b) If you can vary the direction of the proton's velocity, find the magnitude of the maximum and minimum forces you could achieve, and show how the velocity should be oriented to achieve these forces. (c) What would the answers to part (a) be if the proton were replaced by an electron traveling in the same way as the proton?
expectedAnswer: a) 3.54 × 10⁻¹⁶ N, into the page  b) F_max = 4.32 × 10⁻¹⁶ N, F_min = 0  c) 3.54 × 10⁻¹⁶ N, out of the page

parts.label: a
parts.question: Find the magnitude and direction of the force this magnetic field exerts on the proton.

parts.label: b
parts.question: If you can vary the direction of the proton's velocity, find the magnitude of the maximum and minimum forces you could achieve, and show how the velocity should be oriented to achieve these forces.

parts.label: c
parts.question: What would the answers to part (a) be if the proton were replaced by an electron traveling in the same way as the proton?

questionText: A particle having a mass of $0.195\mathrm{g}$ carries a charge of $-2.50\times 10^{-8}\mathrm{C}$. The particle is given an initial horizontal northward velocity of $4.00\times 10^{4}\mathrm{m / s}$. What are the magnitude and direction of the minimum magnetic field that will balance the earth's gravitational pull on the particle?

At a given instant, a particle with a mass of $5.00 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$ and a charge of $3.50 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ has a velocity with a magnitude of $2.00 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~m/s}$ in the $+y$ direction. It is moving in a uniform magnetic field that has magnitude $0.8 \mathrm{T}$ and is in the $-x$ direction. What are (a) the magnitude and direction of the magnetic force on the particle and (b) its resulting acceleration?
expectedAnswer: a) 5.60 × 10⁻³ N, +z direction  b) 1.12 m/s², +z direction

parts.label: a
parts.question: the magnitude and direction of the magnetic force on the particle

parts.label: b
parts.question: its resulting acceleration?

questionText: If the magnitude of the magnetic force on a proton is $F$ when it is moving at $15.0^{\circ}$ with respect to the field, what is the magnitude of the force (in terms of $F$) when this charge is moving at $30.0^{\circ}$ with respect to the field?

A particle with mass $3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~kg}$ and charge $+5 \mu \mathrm{C}$ enters a region of space where there is a magnetic field of $1 \mathrm{T}$ that is perpendicular to the velocity of the particle. When the particle encounters the magnetic field, it experiences an acceleration of $12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. What is the speed of the particle when it enters the magnetic-field region?

A particle with a charge of $-2.50 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ is moving with an instantaneous velocity of magnitude $40.0 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$ in the $x-y$ plane at an angle of $50^{\circ}$ counterclockwise from the $+x$ axis. What are the magnitude and direction of the force exerted on this particle by a magnetic field with magnitude $2.00 \mathrm{T}$ in the (a) $-x$ direction and (b) $+z$ direction?
expectedAnswer: a) 1.53 × 10⁻⁵ N, -z direction  b) 2.00 × 10⁻³ N, 50° counterclockwise from the +y axis

parts.label: a
parts.question: $-x$ direction

parts.label: b
parts.question: $+z$ direction?

questionText: A particle with mass $1.81 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$ and a charge of $+1.22 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ has, at a given instant, a velocity of $3.00 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{m/s}$ along the $+y$ axis, as shown in Figure 20.56. What are the magnitude and direction of the particle's acceleration produced by a magnetic field of magnitude $1.25 \mathrm{T}$ in the $x-y$ plane, directed at an angle of $45.0^{\circ}$ counterclockwise from the $+x$ axis?

A $150\mathrm{V}$ battery is connected across two parallel metal plates of area $28.5\mathrm{cm}^2$ and separation $8.20\mathrm{mm}$. A beam of alpha particles (charge $+2e$, mass $6.64\times 10^{-27}\mathrm{kg}$) is accelerated from rest through a potential difference of $1.75\mathrm{kV}$ and enters the region between the plates perpendicular to the electric field. What magnitude and direction of magnetic field are needed so that the alpha particles emerge undeflected from between the plates?
